evidence_spans.evidence_span: China AI Data Center Grid Locks Out Nvidia With $295 Billion Domestic Chip Mandate Tech Science Business Health Culture Deals & Reviews Sign In Account Manage Account Logout Home Business Tech China AI Data Center Grid Locks Out Nvidia With $295 Billion Domestic Chip Mandate Huawei fills Nvidia’s slot, but SMIC’s 7nm ceiling and power-grid gaps threaten the deadline By Jerry Owens Published: Jun 22 2026, 2:29 PM EDT Share on Facebook Share on Twitter Share on LinkedIn Share on Reddit Share on Flipboard Share on Pocket People visit the Huawei stand during the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (WAIC) at the Shanghai World Expo and Conven

Getty Images/HECTOR RETAMAL China is drafting the largest single-nation AI infrastructure commitment in history — a five-year, 2 trillion yuan ($295 billion) program to connect thousands of data centers into a unified national computing grid, with a mandate that at least 80% of the underlying technology, including AI accelerator chips, come from domestic suppliers.

A report published today by The Next Web found that Chinese data-center power demand is projected to rise by 300 to 500 billion kilowatt-hours between 2026 and 2030 — roughly a fifth of China's total electricity demand growth over that period — and that operators are struggling to reconcile the load spikes from AI training runs with clean-energy mandates and reliable grid supply.
Whether the hardware and the electricity to power it can materialize on time is a separate and increasingly contested question.

evidence_spans.evidence_span: Lam Research Q1 2026 Earnings Review - Earnings Lam Research (LRCX) Q1 2026 earnings review Period ended Sep 28, 2025 · Filed Oct 22, 2025 Sources: Earnings Release (SEC) , Presentation , Release Record Quarter Shadowed by Margin Contraction and New China Restrictions Lam Research reported a stellar quarter with record revenue of $5.3 billion and a record non-GAAP operating margin of 35.0%, driven by a surge in China sales which accounted for 43% of the total.
Guidance for the December quarter points to a sharp contraction in profitability, with gross margin expected to fall over 200 basis points to 48.5% due to a less favorable customer mix and tariffs.

Furthermore, the company quantified the impact of new US export controls, projecting a ~$200 million revenue hit in the December quarter and a substantial ~$600 million headwind for calendar year 2026, signaling that the current peak in performance is unlikely to be sustained.

Either infrastructure stocks are too expensive, or core AI hardware names are still undervalued relative to the scale of demand investors are expecting.

evidence_spans.evidence_span: SNDK — FinLab Research Report 📈 SNDK — FinLab Research Report Run 215c9c25-515f-4155-b851-8844ad5589cd · 2026-05-14 04:11 UTC · Data 2025-02-01 → 2026-05-01 🔬 Executive Summary Consensus View 🔵 MIXED 52% confidence All three analysts agree that SanDisk's underlying business has undergone a genuine and dramatic transformation, driven by AI memory demand, with elite-tier margins, explosive revenue acceleration, and a debt-free balance sheet.

However, the consensus view diverges sharply on risk/reward: the aggressive analyst sees sufficient fundamental momentum to justify a bull stance, while both the neutral and conservative analysts conclude that after a ~4,000% price appreciation in roughly 15 months, the margin of safety has been entirely eliminated.

The dominant source of disagreement is interpretive — whether exceptional fundamentals justify continued exposure at extreme valuations or whether the price already fully discounts even optimistic multi-year scenarios.

The absence of traditional valuation anchors (P/E, P/B) and a material rise in short interest are cited by two of three analysts as disqualifying factors for a constructive position, producing a mixed consensus with below-average agreement and moderate analytical confidence.
